context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class about neoliberal views of the state vs. the East Asian model. I’m kind of confused about how to compare state intervention, civil society/labor/democracy, and then connect that to new institutionalist theory.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's start at the beginning. Before we dig into comparisons, what do you already know or think about the neoliberal vision of the state? What role does it assign to government in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and has given a broad overview of their confusion. Rather than scaffolding all parts of the prompt at once, I should start by probing what they already know about the neoliberal view of the state, which is the first question in the prompt. This will help me gauge their baseline understanding and let them build from there using guided discovery. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think neoliberalism sees the state as supposed to stay pretty limited in the economy, like protect property rights, enforce contracts, keep markets open, and not do too much direct planning. I’m not fully sure if that means no intervention at all, or just only certain kinds.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good starting point. Your instinct about 'certain kinds' of intervention is worth pursuing. Think about it this way: what's the difference between a state that *sets the rules of the game* versus one that *plays the game itself*? How might that distinction map onto what you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid basic grasp of the neoliberal state. Their uncertainty about 'no intervention vs. certain kinds' is a productive tension worth exploring - it shows they're thinking carefully. I should affirm the good thinking and push them to sharpen that distinction rather than resolving it for them.

exchanges.student: I think “sets the rules” means the state creates a legal framework and stable conditions for markets, but mostly lets private firms make the real economic decisions. “Plays the game” sounds more like the state actively directing investment or owning industries, which feels less neoliberal.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp distinction. Now, with that framework in mind, how would you describe what states like South Korea, Taiwan, or Japan actually did in their development periods? Were they 'setting rules' or 'playing the game' — or something else entirely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a clean and accurate distinction. This is good progress. Now I should pivot them toward the East Asian case, which is the next part of the prompt, using their own framing as a bridge. I want them to discover the contrast themselves.

exchanges.student: I think they were doing both, which is where I get a little stuck. They weren’t fully socialist, but they also weren’t just passive referees—they used industrial policy, protected some industries, pushed exports, and worked closely with firms, so the state seemed way more directive.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually landed on something important — that tension you're feeling IS the point. So if East Asian states were neither purely socialist nor purely neoliberal referees, what would you call that kind of state? What term or concept might capture a state that is deeply involved in directing the economy but still working *through* markets rather than replacing them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the hybrid nature of the East Asian developmental state. Their confusion about 'both' is actually the key insight - this is exactly what makes East Asia theoretically interesting and distinct from the neoliberal model. I should validate this and push them to name what that model is called, or at least articulate what makes it distinctive, rather than giving them the term.

exchanges.student: Is that basically the “developmental state”? I think that means the state is strong and interventionist, but it still uses markets as tools instead of trying to eliminate them.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now here's a trickier question: the prompt asks about the role of civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. What do you know or suspect about how much space these states gave to labor unions, democratic participation, or civil society groups during their high-growth periods?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the developmental state concept. Now I should move them toward the next part of the prompt: the role of civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. This is often a surprising or counterintuitive part of the story, so I want to let them discover it through questioning rather than explaining it.

exchanges.student: My impression is that there often wasn’t much space, at least during the high-growth phase. I think a lot of those states were pretty authoritarian, and labor was controlled or suppressed so wages stayed competitive, but I’m less sure how to talk about civil society without oversimplifying it.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is solid. So what does that suggest about the relationship between state-led development and democracy? Does the East Asian case challenge or support the idea that economic development and political freedom go hand in hand?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good grasp of the authoritarian dimension and labor suppression. Their hesitation about civil society shows good intellectual caution. Rather than resolving it for them, I should push them to think about what this means theoretically - specifically, what it implies about the relationship between development and democracy, which is a central tension in the prompt and in the literature.

exchanges.student: I think it kind of challenges that idea, at least in the short term. The East Asian cases make it seem like rapid development can happen under authoritarian or semi-authoritarian states, so economic growth and political freedom don’t automatically advance together.
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking. Now let's tackle the last piece: new institutionalism. What do you know about that theory, and why do you think the prompt asks you to consider the East Asian experience *in the context of* new institutionalist theories?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached a nuanced and accurate observation about the decoupling of economic development and political liberalization. This is good. We've now covered neoliberalism, the developmental state, and civil society/democracy reasonably well. The prompt's last question about new institutionalism hasn't been touched yet, and we're about 6 exchanges in. I should pivot to that now to make sure they can connect it in their reflection.

exchanges.student: I’m a little fuzzy on new institutionalism. I think it says institutions matter a lot for development—like not just markets by themselves, but the rules, organizations, and state capacity behind them. So maybe the East Asian case matters because it shows strong institutions and state capacity were a big reason intervention actually worked?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong connection. You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt. I think it's time to start putting this together. Can you write a short paragraph — even just a rough draft — that sets up the contrast between the neoliberal state and the East Asian developmental state? Don't worry about polish, just get your ideas down.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a very good intuitive connection between the East Asian developmental state and new institutionalism. Their link between state capacity, institutions, and successful intervention is exactly right. We've now covered all major parts of the prompt across about 7 exchanges. Given the rules about redundancy and the conversation length target of 10 exchanges, I should now push the student to start integrating everything into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss individual concepts.

exchanges.student: Okay, rough version: Neoliberal theory imagines the state mainly as a regulator that creates the conditions for markets to function efficiently, such as protecting property rights, enforcing contracts, and limiting distortions. By contrast, the East Asian developmental state took a much more active role in shaping economic change through industrial policy, export promotion, credit allocation, and close coordination with firms. It did not replace markets, but it also did not treat markets as self-sufficient, which makes it a challenge to a strict neoliberal view.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid paragraph — the contrast is clear and your framing of markets as 'not self-sufficient' is well put. Now, your reflection still needs to bring in civil society, labor, and democracy, and then connect to new institutionalism. Can you try drafting a sentence or two on each of those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely good opening paragraph that captures the core contrast clearly and accurately. I should acknowledge this positively but push them forward to integrate the civil society/labor/democracy dimension and the new institutionalism piece into their draft, since those are still missing from the written work.

exchanges.student: Maybe for civil society/labor/democracy I’d say that East Asian growth was often tied to limited labor power and constrained democratic participation, which suggests the developmental state was not just economic but also political. And for new institutionalism, I’d say these cases show that development depends on capable institutions and state-business coordination, not just on whether the state intervenes or stays out.
exchanges.tutor: Good progress — both additions work. One thing to sharpen: your new institutionalism sentence says institutions matter, but can you push a little further? What specifically about institutions — their quality, their design, their embeddedness in political context — helps explain *why* East Asian intervention succeeded where similar attempts elsewhere failed? That distinction could make your reflection more analytical.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has drafted good sentences for both remaining components. The civil society/labor/democracy piece correctly identifies the political dimension. The new institutionalism connection is accurate but could be sharper - specifically, they could push on what new institutionalism says about WHY institutions matter (not just that they do). However, given we're at exchange 9 and nearing the end of the planned conversation, I should encourage them to integrate all parts into a coherent draft and flag one concrete area for improvement without over-scaffolding.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently asking questions that guided the student to derive key concepts independently. No submission-ready content was provided; the student generated all core ideas. Scaffolding was well-sequenced, building from neoliberal basics to the developmental state concept to civil society/democracy to new institutionalism. The final turn pushed the student to deepen their analysis rather than simply affirming their draft. No off-topic drift, no redundancy, and tone was consistently supportive and formative.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student answers directly — concepts like 'developmental state,' the neoliberal/East Asian contrast, and the new institutionalism connection were all elicited through questioning. The student's draft paragraphs were student-generated, and the tutor's feedback in Turn 10 pushed for deeper analysis rather than providing it. No near-submission-ready content was produced by the tutor. 1.2: The tutor built on each student response sequentially, acknowledged correct steps (e.g., 'That's a sharp distinction,' 'Exactly right'), and progressed logically through the prompt's components. 1.3: The final turn provided specific methodological feedback — asking the student to distinguish institutional quality, design, and embeddedness — which is process-level guidance. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift occurred. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and well-targeted; tone was warm, encouraging, and clearly formative throughout.
